era_context: The Classical Greek world in the early 4th century BCE, following the end of the Peloponnesian War (404 BCE). Athens is recovering politically and culturally under democratic restoration; Sparta dominates mainland Greece but faces challenges to its hegemony. In the Near East, the Persian Achaemenid Empire under Artaxerxes II remains the dominant regional power. In Italy, Rome is consolidating control over the Latin League and beginning expansion into central Italy.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Northern Europe beyond Greek/Roman contact zones

events.title: Sack of Rome by Gallic forces under Brennus
events.description: A coalition of Celtic (Gallic) tribes, led by the chieftain Brennus, invaded central Italy and defeated a Roman army at the Battle of the Allia River. The Gauls subsequently sacked the city of Rome itself, burning much of it and forcing the Roman population to retreat to the Capitoline Hill. The city was ransomed with gold, and the Gauls eventually withdrew. This event shocked the Mediterranean world and demonstrated Rome's vulnerability, though it ultimately prompted military and defensive reforms that strengthened Roman institutions.
key_figures: Camillus (Roman military leader, though his role in this year is debated)

sources.name: Livy, History of Rome (Ab urbe condita), Books 5–6

events.certainty_note: The sack of Rome is attested in multiple later sources (Livy, Diodorus, Plutarch) but no contemporary written records survive. Archaeological evidence for destruction in Rome around this period is debated; some scholars place the event slightly later (c. 387 BCE) or question its scale. The date 390 BCE follows the traditional chronology but is not definitively confirmed by archaeology.
cross_references: Follows from broader Celtic migrations into Italy in the 5th–4th centuries BCE
Led to Roman military reforms and the construction of the Servian Wall

events.title: Artaxerxes II's consolidation of Persian control in Egypt
events.description: Following the Egyptian revolt under Amyrtaeus (404–399 BCE), Persian forces under Artaxerxes II reasserted direct control over Egypt, ending the brief period of Egyptian independence. This reassertion of Persian hegemony was part of Artaxerxes II's broader consolidation of imperial authority after the Anabasis of Cyrus the Younger (401 BCE) and internal instability. Egypt remained under Persian rule until the Macedonian conquest under Alexander the Great.
key_figures: Phanes of Halicarnassus (Greek mercenary commander, possibly involved in reconquest)

sources.name: Egyptian demotic papyri and administrative records

events.certainty_note: The reconquest of Egypt by Artaxerxes II is attested in Greek sources and Egyptian administrative records. The precise date is uncertain; some sources place it around 390 BCE, others around 387 BCE. The process may have taken several years.
cross_references: Follows from the Egyptian revolt of 404–399 BCE
Part of Artaxerxes II's reign (404–358 BCE)

events.title: Corinthian War: ongoing conflict between Sparta and anti-Spartan coalition
events.description: The Corinthian War (395–387 BCE) was in its middle phase, with Thebes, Corinth, Athens, and Argos allied against Spartan hegemony. Naval and land campaigns continued across Greece. This conflict was partly fueled by Persian gold supporting anti-Spartan factions, as Artaxerxes II sought to weaken Spartan power in the Aegean. The war would eventually be resolved by the King's Peace (Peace of Antalcidas) in 387 BCE.
key_figures: Conon (Athenian admiral, supported by Persian gold)
events.certainty_note: Xenophon's Hellenica is a near-contemporary account by a participant in Greek affairs. The Corinthian War is well-attested in multiple sources.
cross_references: Part of the broader Corinthian War (395–387 BCE)
Led to the King's Peace of 387 BCE

disconfirming_evidence: The dating and scale of the Gallic sack of Rome (390-001) is disputed by modern scholarship. Some scholars, based on archaeological evidence and analysis of later sources, argue the sack occurred around 387 BCE rather than 390 BCE, or that it was less catastrophic than later Roman sources (Livy, Plutarch) suggest. The absence of contemporary written records and the reliance on sources written centuries later (Livy wrote in the Augustan age) introduces significant uncertainty. Additionally, some scholars question whether the sack was as complete or destructive as tradition claims, noting that Rome recovered remarkably quickly if the destruction was truly total.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 390 BCE is heavily shaped by later Greek and Roman historiography. For Rome, we rely almost entirely on Livy and other sources written 300+ years after the events, creating substantial risk of legendary embellishment or distortion. For Greece, Xenophon's Hellenica provides near-contemporary narrative, but it reflects an Athenian/pro-Spartan perspective and omits much of the broader Mediterranean world. Persian sources are sparse; Egyptian administrative records exist but are fragmentary. The record is heavily weighted toward Mediterranean and Near Eastern events; sub-Saharan Africa, northern Europe, and Asia beyond Persian reach are essentially absent from surviving documentation.

graph_edges.note: The Gallic sack prompted Roman military reforms and defensive construction
Reassertion of Persian control followed the Egyptian revolt of 404–399 BCE
The Corinthian War was resolved by the King's Peace of 387 BCE
